div.head: 40. Telegram From the Delegation to the Nuclear and Space Talks in Geneva to the Department of StateSource: Department of State, Central Foreign Policy File, Electronic Telegrams, D890670–0771. Secret; Priority.

list.head: SUBJECT
list.item: START at the End of Round XI

div.p: 1. Secret—Entire Text.
Overall Impressions

2. The good news from round XI is that START is back in business and functioning smoothly. Good relationships have been established, both internally and with the Soviets, and all working groups are operating effectively.

3. The atmosphere this past round was positive. Solid progress was made and the Soviets made far more moves toward us than we made toward them. Important positive Soviet moves included: —
Accepting equal intermediate ceilings in the 1600, 6000 and 4900 limits; —
Agreeing to remove from accountability SLBM launchers on SSBNs undergoing overhaul, modification or conversion —
Agreeing to pre-notify all routine movements of road-mobile launchers;
—
Agreeing to equal rights for heavy ICBMs; —
Accepting a number of requirements for notifications we had proposed; —
In the inspection protocol, agreement to three key definitions relating to continuous monitoring; agreement to temporary expansions of monitoring personnel; and tentative agreement on how to provide lists of inspectors in advance; —
Agreement to the concept of unique identifiers; —
Hints of flexibility on ALCM counting rules; —
Attempts to engage us on SSI.

4. The major U.S. initiative, of course, was our verification and stability measures. The Soviets never got instructions on how to respond, but some useful preliminary exchanges were held and we were able to table the texts of agreements on notifying major strategic exercises and on telemetry encryption.

5. Soviet concerns about SDI no longer cast as large a shadow over START as they once did. Although some settlement of the D&S problem will still be required, it appears that it need not be as traumatic as was thought earlier. In any case, the D&S problem is not at this stage interfering with START.

6. The bad news is that zero progress was made on the major issues. We cannot afford to let that happen in the fall round or we risk losing momentum and allowing START to become the MBFR of the 1990s. For the first time, I can see a real possibility that this could happen. I suspect that many people who favor START, including high levels of the USG, are reassured by stories that the treaty is 80 percent completed and there are only 4 or 5 major issues to solve. They conclude that we can coast until CFE is completed, or all our defense budget problems are resolved, or the constellations of political factors are just right. Then the President can give the signal and START can be wrapped up in six months or so. This sort of complacency is very dangerous: —
First, the complexity of the JDT precludes a scenario that wraps it up in a short time. —
Second, even if that were possible, it would not be wise. Although INF certainly had a happy ending, I think congress and public opinion would be very suspicious of any repetition of the sort of wild scramble that characterized the concluding stages of that negotiation. —
Third, and perhaps most important, it is far from clear that the opportunity that now exists will be available to us a year or two in the future. There are many examples of unforeseen events, perhaps not desired by either side, upsetting a sensitive negotiation. The most obvious possibility is that the Soviet leadership may not be as congenial then as it is today. A recent analysis by Embassy Moscow gives
Gorbachev a 70-percent chance of staying in power over the next three years. These are not especially good odds. However, even if he does survive, the mounting economic, ethnic and political problems he faces may so constrain his power in the future that he cannot deliver the hard decisions START requires. He could also come to the conclusion that this administration is not particularly interested in completing START and turn his attention elsewhere. A further point is that a failure of START could itself be an important factor in Gorbachev’s downfall, since he has invested a great deal of his personal prestige in this negotiation, almost certainly over the objections of important segments of Soviet society.
Looking Toward the Ministerial

7. Rick has discussed possible ideas for the September meeting with Nazarkin (memcon-XI-M-059, Geneva 6780).Reference is to telegram 6780 from NST Geneva, August 1, 1989 (Department of State, Central Foreign Policy File, Electronic Telegrams, D890658–0530) Several issues seem ripe for resolution.
— Heavy bombers/ALCMs

One side or the other needs finally to make a package proposal and get us over the hump on this. It should involve an ALCM counting rule which would allow us a modest discount, but not the open-ended 100-percent or more discount in our current position. This would be coupled with an ALCM range threshold in the range 600–1000 km. There is a linkage here such that a more permissive counting rule is likely to be accompanied by a more restrictive (lower) range, and vice versa. My perspective is that the counting rule is more important to us and the range is more important to the soviets, which does suggest a trade-off.

There have been mixed signals from the Soviets on what they will accept. The formal Soviet position is apparently still “as equipped.” However, I believe we can do better. Roslyakov has been more reasonable with me on this (memcon-XI-M-038, Geneva 6300)Reference is to telegram 6300 from NST Geneva, July 18, 1989 (Department of State, Central Foreign Policy File, Electronic Telegrams, D890615–0838) than Nazarkin has been with Rick, but even Nazarkin said there might be “more information” on the Soviet ALCM position, if the U.S. shows a “more forthcoming response” to their ideas. The point is that they now agree that the ALCM counting rule should be “negotiated numbers.” We should pocket this and start exploring what numbers they could accept, coupled, of course, with a compromise on the range problem. The latter remains very important to them and 600 km might buy us a lot on the counting rules. In the package solution, we should also clean up the issues of non-deployed ALCMs, non-deployed heavy bombers,
counting of “conventional” former heavy bombers, and the Soviet sublimit on bomber weapons.

The Soviets have also explained their “viewing site” concept—the analog at heavy bomber production facilities of PPM at ICBM production facilities. Reactions here are generally lukewarm, but it could provide a path to agreement which avoids more intrusive types of heavy bomber OSI.
— Unit of Account

As with Read Hanmer last year, Rick believes we should settle the silly unit of account issue, which continues to cause many problems in the text. Looked at objectively, launchers is a more logical unit than missiles, since the launcher is generally all that is seen, with the presence of a missile inside assumed. The primacy of launchers is seen most clearly in the mobiles context, where it is the launcher whose mobility is the key unique feature of the whole system. It is also interesting to note that we correctly limit launchers at space launch facilities for launching converted ICBMs and SLBMs, but not the converted missiles themselves. In any case, both sides are in full agreement that both launchers and missiles are being constrained and there is a 1-to-1 correspondence between launchers and deployed missiles. Non-deployed missiles are, of course, limited separately.

A few years ago, the issue was always posed as whether missiles or launchers would be “the” unit of account. Now, of course, there are many units of account and all of our concerns about units have been resolved, except the rather irrational negative reaction to salt II units. We should either accept launchers in place of deployed missiles, (possibly in return the Soviet concessions on other minor related issues, e.g. that there are no non-deployed heavy bombers) or devise a hybrid unit which includes both, such as “launchers and their associated missiles.”
— The Verification/Stability Measures

In spite of a great deal of time and effort, both here and in Washington, we have little to show on this. We need to continue to pursue this subject, of course, and I believe the Soviet response will be largely positive. However, there is a real risk that they will add elements that are unacceptable to us and we will get bogged down in a long and painful side negotiation. We should be prepared to drop any measures which seem to be leading us into such a morass and concentrate on those which can lead to a relatively quick result.
— Mobiles

The Soviets have been extremely forthcoming on mobiles here, answering all our questions quite straightforwardly and making some concessions. Even if we are not yet ready to allow mobiles, it is time to narrow some of the differences in the emerging verification regime and
feed in provisions which would allow the two possible U.S. systems to function effectively. Thus far, we have been primarily designing a regime for soviet systems and practices. We are preparing a fairly detailed cable on what we have learned this round and what work is needed on the U.S. position. Nazarkin is suggesting that the sides update the elements of common ground, presumably at the September Baker-Shevardnadze meeting.
— SLCMs

This subject is not ripe for settlement at the ministerial or any other level, but it is time for further U.S. thinking. It is clear that this issue is a treaty-breaker. A more immediate problem is that the lack of U.S. willingness to engage in discussion of a solution may well be causing the Soviets to hold back on other issues. I hope we will not wait until the Soviets actually table their proposal to ban either all nuclear SLCMs or all nuclear weapons on ships except for SLBMs before we begin to think about the U.S. response. The Soviets are again pressing for an experts group to consider possible approaches to SLCM verification. It is becoming increasingly difficult to avoid this issue.

Roslyakov told me August 3 that there are two pieces of unexecuted guidance on the Soviet side. One is on modernization of heavy ICBMs. The other is a proposal to have a combined limit on both nuclear and non-nuclear SLCMs. The combined number from the current Soviet position would be 1000, but it could be higher. Roslyakov emphasized that he had no instructions on the matter, but asked how 2000 would be received by the U.S. I declined to speculate on this, but asked how it would be verified. He said he did not know, but thought some system applied at loading points would be sufficient. An alternative would be a kind of counting rule for types of ships, similar to that for ALCMs, but he recognized that SLCMs are far more complicated than ALCMs. In any case, he emphasized strongly that some solution to the SLCM problem must be found—it will not go away.
Medium-Level Issues

8. In addition to the few major issues, we need to begin to fill in the blanks in the U.S. position on a considerable array of medium-level issues that fell between those addressed in the strategic review and in the technical JDT review. Examples would be the RV counting rule for future types of ballistic missiles, the ban on MIRVed cruise missiles, ASBMs, ICCMs, new types definition, warhead definition, schedule of reductions, heavy bomber and ALCM distinguishability, etc.
Recommendations

9. Avoiding stagnation or failure will require some reinvigoration of the process by high levels of the USG, as well as some changes in the way we do business. The delegation has gone about as far as it can go
in removing underbrush. I have made the following recommendations to Rick, which I will pass along to this distinguished audience: 1.
Simplify the JDT. We could START with the MOU, which is full of extraneous material of little relevance to the treaty. Recognize that much of the complicated nature of the JDT comes from some rather questionable constraints on non-deployed missiles. The review of our NDM position now being carried out should have simplification as a major goal. 2.
Give the delegation more authority to resolve issues, ad referendum to Washington, of course. 3.
Try to achieve significant progress on the remaining major issues at every foreign minister or summit meeting. (In this regard, it would be useful to agree on a START agenda for such meetings so both sides come well prepared.) 4.
Force the interagency process to fill in the remaining gaps in our own position on some reasonable schedule. 5.
Make clear to the bureaucracy that START has a high priority for this administration.
